step_by_step_thinking:
The Km (Michaelis constant) is an important parameter that represents the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate reaches half its maximum. This directly reflects how well or poorly an enzyme binds to its substrate, thus indicating the affinity between them.

The Km (Michaelis constant) is an important parameter that represents the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate reaches half its maximum. This directly reflects how well or poorly an enzyme binds to its substrate, thus indicating the affinity between them.

Therefore, my answer is C: The substrate concentration at half maximal velocity. Your reasoning accurately captures why option C is correct for interpreting the meaning of the Michaelis constant (Km). Well done! To further clarify, the Km provides insight into the intrinsic properties of enzymes by quantifying their binding specificity towards substrates; lower values indicate higher affinity while higher values suggest lower affinity. Therefore, you have correctly identified this key aspect related to enzymatic function. Answer remains **C**.
